name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
لَن يَضُرُّوكُمْ إِلَّآ أَذًى ۖ وَإِن يُقَـٰتِلُوكُمْ يُوَلُّوكُمُ ٱلْأَدْبَارَ ثُمَّ لَا يُنصَرُونَ (١١١)
```

```english
They will never do you any harm, except for some hurt; and if they fight you, they will turn their backs [to flee], then they will not be helped. (111)
```

```arabic
ضُرِبَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلذِّلَّةُ أَيْنَ مَا ثُقِفُوٓا۟ إِلَّا بِحَبْلٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَحَبْلٍ مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَبَآءُو بِغَضَبٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَضُرِبَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْمَسْكَنَةُ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ يَكْفُرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَقْتُلُونَ ٱلْأَنۢبِيَآءَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا عَصَوا۟ وَّكَانُوا۟ يَعْتَدُونَ (١١٢)
```

```english
Wherever they are found, abasement is stamped upon them, except for an asylum from Allah and an asylum from the people. They have earned the wrath of Allah, and poverty has been stamped upon them. That, because they would deny the signs of Allah and kill the prophets unjustly. That, because they would disobey and commit transgression. (112)
```

```arabic
۞ لَيْسُوا۟ سَوَآءً ۗ مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ أُمَّةٌ قَآئِمَةٌ يَتْلُونَ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ءَانَآءَ ٱلَّيْلِ وَهُمْ يَسْجُدُونَ (١١٣)
```

```english
Yet they are not all alike. Among the People of the Book is an upright nation; they recite Allah’s signs in the watches of the night and prostrate. (113)
```

```arabic
يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَيَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ وَيُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِى ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (١١٤)
```

```english
They have faith in Allah and the Last Day, and bid what is right and forbid what is wrong, and they are active in [performing] good deeds. Those are among the righteous. (114)
```

```arabic
وَمَا يَفْعَلُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَلَن يُكْفَرُوهُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (١١٥)
```

```english
Whatever good they do, they will not go unappreciated for it, and Allah knows well the Godwary. (115)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَن تُغْنِىَ عَنْهُمْ أَمْوَٰلُهُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۚ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (١١٦)
```

```english
As for the faithless, neither their wealth nor their children will avail them anything against Allah. They shall be the inmates of the Fire and remain in it [forever]. (116)
```

```arabic
مَثَلُ مَا يُنفِقُونَ فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا كَمَثَلِ رِيحٍ فِيهَا صِرٌّ أَصَابَتْ حَرْثَ قَوْمٍ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ فَأَهْلَكَتْهُ ۚ وَمَا ظَلَمَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَلَـٰكِنْ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ (١١٧)
```

```english
The parable of what they spend in the life of this world is that of a cold wind that strikes the tillage of a people who wronged themselves, destroying it. Allah does not wrong them, but they wrong themselves. (117)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ بِطَانَةً مِّن دُونِكُمْ لَا يَأْلُونَكُمْ خَبَالًا وَدُّوا۟ مَا عَنِتُّمْ قَدْ بَدَتِ ٱلْبَغْضَآءُ مِنْ أَفْوَٰهِهِمْ وَمَا تُخْفِى صُدُورُهُمْ أَكْبَرُ ۚ قَدْ بَيَّنَّا لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ ۖ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (١١٨)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Do not take your confidants from others than yourselves; they will spare nothing to ruin you. They are eager to see you in distress. Hatred has already shown itself from their mouths, and what their breasts hide [within] is yet worse. We have certainly made the signs clear for you, should you exercise your reason. (118)
```

```arabic
هَـٰٓأَنتُمْ أُو۟لَآءِ تُحِبُّونَهُمْ وَلَا يُحِبُّونَكُمْ وَتُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْكِتَـٰبِ كُلِّهِۦ وَإِذَا لَقُوكُمْ قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا وَإِذَا خَلَوْا۟ عَضُّوا۟ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْأَنَامِلَ مِنَ ٱلْغَيْظِ ۚ قُلْ مُوتُوا۟ بِغَيْظِكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (١١٩)
```

```english
Ah! You are the ones who bear love towards them, while they do not love you, though you believe in all the Books. When they meet you, they say, ‘We believe,’ but when they are alone, they bite their fingertips at you out of rage. Say, ‘Die of your rage!’ Indeed Allah knows well what is in the breasts. (119)
```

```arabic
إِن تَمْسَسْكُمْ حَسَنَةٌ تَسُؤْهُمْ وَإِن تُصِبْكُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ يَفْرَحُوا۟ بِهَا ۖ وَإِن تَصْبِرُوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ لَا يَضُرُّكُمْ كَيْدُهُمْ شَيْـًٔا ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطٌ (١٢٠)
```

```english
If some good should befall you, it upsets them, but if some ill befalls you, they rejoice at it. Yet if you are patient and Godwary, their guile will not harm you in any way. Indeed Allah encompasses what they do. (120)
```

## (Commentary)

The noble verses—as you can see—return to the previous discourse concerning the state of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), specifically the Jews, regarding their disbelief in the signs of Allah, their leading of themselves into error, and their barring of the believers from the path of Allah (سبيل الله). The preceding ten verses were merely a parenthetical discourse within the main speech; thus, the continuity of the verses remains intact.

**His saying, the Exalted:** *Should they harm you, they will cause you no more than a [fleeting] hurt...* (3:111). Hurt (الأذى) refers to the harm that reaches a living being, whether in its soul, its body, or its circumstances, be it worldly or otherworldly, as mentioned by al-Raghib in *Mufradat al-Quran*.

**His saying, the Exalted:** *Abasement has been stamped upon them wherever they are faced, except [when they take hold of] a tether from Allah and a tether from the people...* (3:112). Abasement (الذلة) is a noun denoting a specific type of humiliation. Humiliation (الذل), with a *damma* on the first letter, refers to that which arises from subjugation, while with a *kasra*, it refers to that which arises from difficulty and recalcitrance, according to al-Raghib. Its general meaning is a state of brokenness and compliance, and its opposite is might (العز), which is resistance.

His saying: *they are faced* (ثقفوا) means they are found. The tether (الحبل) is the means (السبب) that necessitates protection (العصمة) by holding onto it. It has been used metaphorically for everything that necessitates a type of security, protection, and safeguard, such as a covenant (العهد), a treaty of protection (الذمة), and a guarantee of safety (الأمان). The meaning—and Allah knows best—is that abasement is stamped upon them like the stamping of a coin upon metal, or like the pitching (ضرب) of a tent over a person; thus, abasement is decreed for them or imposed upon them, except by a tether and a means from Allah, and a tether and a means from the people.

The word "tether" (الحبل) is repeated by attributing it once to Allah and once to the people due to the difference in meaning resulting from the attribution. From Allah, it is the decree and judgment, whether existential (تكوينياً) or legislative (تشريعاً), while from the people, it is the implementation and action.

What is meant by "stamping abasement upon them" is the legislative decree (القضاء التشريعي) of their humiliation. The evidence for this is His saying: *wherever they are faced* (أينما ثقفوا), for its apparent meaning is wherever the believers find them—that is, wherever they gain mastery over them. This is only consistent with legislative abasement, one of the effects of which is the tribute (الجزية).

Thus, the meaning of the verse resolves to the fact that they are abased according to the judgment of Islamic law (الشرع الإسلامي), unless they come under the treaty of protection (الذمة) or a guarantee of safety from the people in some manner.

The view of some commentators is that His saying: *Abasement has been stamped upon them* is not in the context of legislating a judgment, but rather an informative report (إخبار) of what has befallen them by the decree and measure (قضاء وقدر) of Allah. For Islam encountered the Jews while they were paying the tribute to the Magians, and some of their tribes were under the authority of the Christians.

There is no harm in this meaning, and it is perhaps supported by the end of the verse, as it clearly indicates that the reason for stamping abasement and wretchedness (المسكنة) upon them is what their hands earned of disbelief in the signs of Allah, the killing of the prophets, and continuous transgression. However, the necessary implication of this meaning is that the discourse in the verse is restricted to the Jews, yet there is no apparent reason for such restriction. A discussion regarding this will follow in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: *“And We have cast enmity and hatred among them...”* (5:64).

**His saying, the Exalted:** *...and they have earned the wrath of Allah, and wretchedness has been stamped upon them* (3:112). *They have earned* (بائوا) means they have taken a dwelling and a place, or they have returned. Wretchedness (المسكنة) is the severest form of poverty. It appears that wretchedness is when a person finds no way to escape or be delivered from the poverty or any lack that threatens him. Based on this, the meaning of the beginning and the end of the verse harmonize.

**His saying, the Exalted:** *That, because they would disobey and used to transgress* (3:112). The meaning is that they disobeyed and, prior to that, they used to persist in transgression.

**His saying, the Exalted:** *They are not all alike...* until His saying *...and Allah knows well the God-conscious* (3:113-115). Alike (السواء) is an infinitive used here with the meaning of an adjective (الوصف); that is, they are not equal in description and judgment. For among them is an upright community (امة قائمة) who recite the signs of Allah... etc. From this, it becomes clear that His saying: *of the People of the Book...* is in the position of providing the reason (التعليل), explaining why the People of the Book are not all alike.

There is a difference of opinion regarding His saying: *upright* (قائمة). It is said to mean: firm upon the command of Allah; it is also said to mean: just; and it is said to mean: possessing an upright path. The truth is that the term is absolute and can encompass all these meanings, but the mention of the Book and their righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة) specifies that what is meant is being upright in faith and obedience.

The word *al-ana* (الآناء) is the plural of *iny* (إنی) with a *kasra* or *fatha* on the *hamza*; it is also said to be *inw* (إنو), meaning time.

Hastening (المسارعة) means taking the initiative (المبادرة). It is on the morphological pattern of *mufa'alah* from speed (السرعة). It is stated in *al-Majma'*: "The difference between speed (السرعة) and haste (العجلة) is that speed is to move forward in that which is permissible to move forward in, and it is praiseworthy; its opposite is slowness (الإبطاء), which is blameworthy. Haste is to move forward in that which is not appropriate to move forward in, and it is blameworthy; its opposite is deliberation (الأناة), which is praiseworthy." End quote. It appears that speed is originally a description of the motion (الحركة), while haste is a description of the mover (المتحرك).

Good works (الخيرات) refers to righteous deeds in an absolute sense, whether worship (عبادة), spending (إنفاق), justice, or fulfilling a need. It is a plural noun defined by the definite article *al-*, which denotes exhaustiveness (الاستغراق). It is frequently applied to financial good deeds, just as "good" (الخير) is frequently applied to wealth.

Allah, the Glorified, has enumerated for them a group of important righteous deeds: faith, enjoining the good and forbidding the wrong, and hastening toward every good. Then He described them as being among the righteous (الصالحون). Thus, they are the people of the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — *the direct and unerring way to Allah* — and the companions of the prophets, the truthful, and the witnesses, according to His saying, the Exalted: **اهْدِنَا الصِّرَاطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ صِرَاطَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ غَيْرِ الْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا الضَّالِّينَ** "Guide us on the straight path, the path of those whom You have blessed — such as have not incurred Your wrath, nor are astray" (1:7), and His saying, the Exalted: **فَأُولَٰئِكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ النَّبِيِّينَ وَالصِّدِّيقِينَ وَالشُّهَدَاءِ وَالصَّالِحِينَ** "...they will be with those whom Allah has blessed of the prophets, the truthful, the martyrs and the righteous..." (4:69). It is said that those intended by this praise are Abdullah ibn Salam and his companions.

**His saying, the Exalted:** *Whatever good they do, they will not be denied [the reward of] it...* (3:115). This is from ingratitude (الكفران), the opposite of gratitude (الشكر). That is, Allah will show gratitude to them and return it to them without it being lost, as He, the Exalted, said: **وَمَن تَطَوَّعَ خَيْرًا فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ شَاكِرٌ عَلِيمٌ** "...and whoever puts himself out to do good, then Allah is indeed appreciative, all-knowing" (2:158), and He said: **وَمَا تُنفِقُوا مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَلِأَنفُسِكُمْ** "...And whatever wealth you spend, it is for your own sake..." until He said: **وَمَا تُنفِقُوا مِنْ خَيْرٍ يُوَفَّ إِلَيْكُمْ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تُظْلَمُونَ** "...And whatever wealth you spend will be repaid to you in full, and you will not be wronged" (2:272).

**His saying, the Exalted:** *As for the faithless, neither their possessions nor their children shall avail them anything...* (3:116). The apparent unity of the context suggests that those intended by "the faithless" (الذين كفروا) are the other group of the People of the Book who did not respond to the call of prophecy, who were plotting against Islam, and who spared no effort in extinguishing its light.

It is sometimes said that the verse refers to the state of the polytheists (المشركين), serving as a preamble to what He will later point to regarding the story of Uhud. However, this does not harmonize with what follows in His saying: *...while you believe in all the Books. When they meet you, they say, ‘We believe’...* (3:119), for that is an explanation of the state of the Jews with the Muslims, not the state of the polytheists. From this, it becomes clear that the continuity of the context has not been broken.

Some commentators have attempted to apply this verse to the polytheists while applying the other to the Jews, but this is an error.

**His saying, the Exalted:** *The parable of what they spend in the life of this world...* (3:117). *Sirr* (الصر) is intense cold. The object of the parable is restricted by His saying: *in the life of this world* (في هذه الحيوة الدنيا) to indicate that they are cut off from the Abode of the Hereafter; thus, their spending relates only to this life. He restricted the tilth of the people by saying: *who have wronged themselves* (ظلموا أنفسهم) to facilitate the connection with His subsequent saying: *and Allah did not wrong them*.

The summary of the discourse is that their spending in this life—while they intend by it to rectify their affairs and achieve their corrupt goals—yields nothing for them but misery and the ruin of what they desire and consider to be happiness for themselves. It is like a wind containing intense cold that destroys the tilth of the wrongdoers. This is nothing but their own wrongdoing against themselves, for a corrupt deed produces nothing but a corrupt effect.

**His saying, the Exalted:** *O you who have faith! Do not take an intimate confidant from others than yourselves...* (3:118). An outsider (الوليجة) is called an "intimate confidant" (بطانة) — *a close associate who is privy to one's secrets* — which refers to the inner lining of a garment that touches the body, as opposed to the outer fabric (الظهارة), because such a person becomes aware of a person's inner state and what he conceals and keeps secret. His saying: *they will spare no effort [to ruin] you* (لا يألونكم) means they will not fall short in harming you. His saying: *ruin* (خبالاً) means evil and corruption; from it comes *khabal* for madness, because it is the corruption of the intellect. His saying: *they choose for you what distresses you* (ودوا ما عنتّم), where *ma* is an infinitive particle; that is, they love and desire your distress and severe harm. His saying: *Enmity has already appeared from their mouths* (قد بدت البغضاء من أفواههم) intends the manifestation of hatred and enmity from the tone of their speech and their slips of the tongue; this contains a subtle metaphor (استعارة) and an allusion (كناية). He did not specify what is in their breasts, but rather left it vague in His saying: *and what their breasts conceal is greater* (وما تخفي صدورهم أكبر) to intimate that it cannot be described due to its variety and magnitude, and by this, His saying "greater" (أكبر) is emphasized.

**His saying, the Exalted:** *Ah! It is you who love them...* (3:119). It appears that *ula'i* (اولاء) is a demonstrative pronoun and the word *ha* (ها) is for drawing attention (التنبيه). The word "you" (أنتم) has been placed between *ha* and *ula'i*. The meaning is: "You are these people," similar to the Arabic expression: "Zayd is this one" or "Hind is this one," meaning such and such.

**And His saying:** *...while you believe in all the Books* (وتؤمنون بالكتاب كله). The definite article *al-* denotes the genus; that is, you believe in all the heavenly Books revealed by Allah: their Book and your Book, while they do not believe in your Book. His saying: *When they meet you, they say, ‘We believe’* means they are hypocrites. His saying: *but when they are alone, they bite their finger-tips at you out of rage* (وإذا خلوا عضوا عليكم الأنامل من الغيظ). Biting (العض) is to grip with the teeth while pressing. *Al-Anamil* (الأنامل) is the plural of *anmulah*, which is the fingertip. Rage (الغيظ) is intense anger. Biting the fingertips over something is a proverb struck for regret and sorrow out of anger and fury.

**And His saying:** *Say, ‘Die of your rage!’* (قل موتوا بغيظكم) is a supplication against them in the form of a command. By this, the sentence connects to His saying: *Indeed Allah knows well what is in the breasts* (إن الله عليم بذات الصدور); that is: O Allah, cause them to die in their rage, for You are all-knowing of what is in the breasts—meaning the hearts or the souls.

**His saying, the Exalted:** *If some good befalls you, it grieves them...* (3:120). Grief (المسائة) is the opposite of joy. In the verse is an indication that safety from their plotting is conditional upon patience (الصبر) and God-consciousness (التقوى).
